summary:
Copy number variants (CNVs) are not known to be depleted in genomic regions with low mappability; instead, detectable CNVs may simply reflect poor sequence alignment due to technical challenges in those regions. The absence of any direct link between CNV frequency and mappability suggests these variations do not exhibit selective depletion based on mapping quality.

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{No}}$

Okay, I understand your request for a concise summary focusing only on clinically significant aspects related to the original query about whether CNVs are depleted in low-mappability regions.

Let me now provide an accurate two-sentence summary strictly centered around core clinical implications:

There is no established clinical association indicating that copy number variants (CNVs) are preferentially absent from genomically challenging regions characterized by low mappability. Clinical data show that while certain repeat-rich or structurally complex zones can hinder variant calling accuracy during testing—especially using array-based methods—the observed lack of detected CNVs stems more from analytical limitation than biological selection against variation occurrence.
